questions.scenario: A script ran a command. You must check whether it succeeded before proceeding.
questions.question: Run 'ls /home/student' then immediately print its exit code using the special variable for it.
questions.description: $? holds the exit code of the most recently executed command. 0 = success. Any non-zero = failure. Must be read immediately — the next command overwrites it.
use_cases: Check if a backup command succeeded
Conditional logic based on command result
Log exit codes for audit trails
hints: Read it immediately after the command

questions.scenario: A script needs to write its PID to a lockfile so another instance can detect it's already running.
questions.question: Print the current shell's PID using the appropriate special variable.
questions.description: $$ is the PID of the current shell process. Essential for creating unique lockfiles (lockfile.$$), temp files that don't collide, and logging which process produced a message.
use_cases: Write PID files for daemon scripts
Identify which script instance produced a log line
hints: The special variable for current PID is $$

questions.scenario: A script expects exactly 2 arguments. It must validate this at startup.
questions.question: Write a one-liner that prints the number of arguments when called as: bash -c 'echo $#' -- arg1 arg2.
questions.description: $# is the count of positional arguments passed to the script or function. Scripts should check $# at the top to validate they received the right number of arguments.
use_cases: Validate argument count at script startup
Provide usage messages when called incorrectly
Branch logic based on how many arguments were given
hints: $# is the argument count special variable
Run it inside bash -c to pass test args

questions.scenario: A script loops over all its arguments. You must use the right special variable to preserve multi-word arguments correctly.
questions.question: Use "$@" to iterate over the arguments 'alpha' 'beta gamma' 'delta' and print each on a line.
questions.description: "$@" expands to each positional argument as a separate word, preserving quoting. "$*" joins all args with $IFS[0]. Always use "$@" in loops — "$*" destroys multi-word argument boundaries.
use_cases: Forward all arguments to another command safely
Process each argument individually in a loop
Preserve filenames with spaces when passing arguments around
hints: "$@" preserves each argument as a separate element
Use a for loop: for arg in "$@"
Run: bash -c 'for a in "$@"; do echo "$a"; done' -- alpha 'beta gamma' delta

validation.command: bash -c 'for a in "$@"; do echo "$a"; done' -- alpha 'beta gamma' delta

questions.scenario: A script needs to generate a random port number between 1024 and 65535 for a test service.
questions.question: Use $RANDOM to generate a number between 1024 and 65535.
questions.description: $RANDOM returns a random integer between 0 and 32767 each time it's referenced. Use arithmetic to scale it. Not cryptographically secure — use /dev/urandom for security-sensitive randomness.
use_cases: Add random delays to avoid thundering herd
Pick a random element from a list in tests
hints: Use $(( 1024 + RANDOM % 64511 )) to scale into range
